0.body: Welcome to the struct-mapper User Guide! struct-mapper provides a powerful derive macro to auto-generate impl From<Source> for Target by mapping struct fields. This eliminates the tedious boilerplate typically associated with struct-to-struct conversions in Rust backend applications.
0.title: Introduction

1.title: Why use struct-mapper?

10.title: Nested Conversions

11.title: Custom Functions

12.title: Combining Attributes

14.body: This page provides a quick summary of all attributes available in struct-mapper.
14.title: Attribute Reference

15.body: Attribute Required Description #[map_from(Type)] Yes (with MapFrom) Specifies the source type for From<Type> generation #[try_map_from(Type)] Yes (with TryMapFrom) Specifies the source type for TryFrom<Type> generation
15.title: Struct-Level Attributes

16.body: Attribute Description #[map(from = "name")] Map from a differently-named source field #[map(skip, default)] Skip this field; use Default::default() #[map(into)] Call .into() on the source field value #[map(with = "path")] Apply a conversion function fn(SourceFieldType) -> TargetFieldType #[map(try_into)] Call .try_into() on the source field value (TryMapFrom only) #[map(try_with = "path")] Apply a fallible function fn(S) -> Result<T, E> (TryMapFrom only) Attributes can be combined, for example: #[map(from = "old", try_with = "parse")]
16.title: Field-Level Attributes

17.body: struct-mapper is built to provide a premium developer experience. A major part of this is providing excellent, span-accurate compile errors when something goes wrong. Instead of vague syntax errors, struct-mapper attempts to identify your intent and guide you to the right solution.
17.title: Error Messages

18.body: Missing #[map_from]: If you derive MapFrom but forget to specify the source struct, you will see exactly what to add and where: error: missing `#[map_from(SourceType)]` attribute. Add `#[map_from(YourSourceStruct)]` to specify which struct to map from. Example: #[derive(MapFrom)] #[map_from(UserEntity)] struct UserResponse { ... } #[map(skip)] without default: struct-mapper forces you to be explicit to avoid accidental behavior. If you want to skip a field, you must explicitly tell it to use a default: error: `#[map(skip)]` requires `#[map(skip, default)]`. When skipping a field, you must provide a default value. Fix: #[map(skip, default)] Contradictory Attributes: If you try to map from a field while also telling the macro to skip it, struct-mapper catches the contradiction: error: `skip` and `from` cannot be used together.
18.title: Examples

2.body: Zero Boilerplate: Focus on your business logic, not copying fields. Zero Runtime Cost: The macro generates the exact same From block you would write by hand. No reflection, no runtime overhead. Clear Error Messages: Built-in safeguards ensure you get perfect, span-accurate compile errors if you make a mistake.
2.title: Benefits

3.body: Adding struct-mapper to your project is straightforward.
3.title: Getting Started

4.body:
Add the crate to your Cargo.toml: [dependencies]
struct-mapper = "0.1" Minimum Supported Rust Version: 1.71.0
4.title: Installation

5.title: Basic Example

6.body: struct-mapper is designed to handle the most common struct conversion scenarios smoothly. Browse through the sections below to see how to handle different field mapping strategies: Basic Mapping - Mapping identical fields. Field Renaming - Dealing with mismatched names. Skip & Default - Handling fields that don't exist in the source. Nested Conversions - Automatically converting child structs. Custom Functions - Applying transformation logic. Combining Attributes - Using multiple mapping strategies at once.
